source.title: Intentional inhibition: how the 'veto-area' exerts control

confidence_rationale: Haggard group fMRI-connectivity study identifying dFMC (dorsal fronto-median cortex / pre-SMA) as the veto locus with top-down suppressive connectivity to premotor cortex. Directly addresses the neural implementation of the 'point of no return' by showing that the veto signal has a specific anatomical seat and a specific connectivity signature. Confidence reduced by the indirect mapping: REE's commit token is a representation problem, not just a location problem.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Voluntary inhibition of a prepared action selectively activates dorsal fronto-median cortex (dFMC/pre-SMA), and effective connectivity analysis reveals that dFMC exerts top-down inhibitory influence over premotor areas. This constitutes a 'veto-area' that mediates intentional suppression of near-complete motor programs.
mapping.ree_translation: The commit token in REE must be cancellable up to a specific window -- the pre-SMA veto window -- after which the motor program becomes irreversible. The existence of a dedicated veto circuit with its own connectivity signature implies that the minimal token contract must include a 'cancellation-window-open' flag that remains active until the point of no return. Once dFMC connectivity to premotor cortex is severed (or in REE terms, once the pre-commit simulation channel closes), the token transitions to a locked-post-commit state. The minimal contract is thus: (planned-at, cancel-open, committed-at).
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper uses a motor preparation paradigm with a specific perceptual cue to inhibit. REE's commit boundary spans cognitive/ethical as well as motor commitments; the dFMC locus may be specific to motor-level veto and may not generalize to E3-level ethical commitment gates. Additionally, 'top-down inhibition of premotor areas' as measured by fMRI effective connectivity is a coarser signal than what REE would need to implement a precise cancel-window flag.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, voluntary action inhibition task, healthy adults, effective connectivity analysis (structural equation modelling)

confidence_components.notes: Haggard lab work on veto is among the most cited in the field. fMRI connectivity does not give timing resolution -- cannot say exactly when the cancel window closes. Transfer risk from motor to cognitive-ethical commits is real.
